Why Does a Floating Action Button Shift Across Devices in React Native? A Permanent Solution with Portal

3 min read
2 hours ago

--

Floating Action Buttons (FABs) are commonly used in mobile applications.
Whether it’s creating new content, opening a filter screen, or triggering a quick action, FABs are usually positioned in the bottom-right corner of the screen.
At first glance, this component seems straightforward. However, consistently positioning it across different devices turned out to be more challenging than I expected.
I started noticing several issues:

• On some Android devices, the button appeared higher than expected.

• On certain iPhone models, it overlapped with the home indicator area.

• It looked correct on screens with long content but shifted upward on shorter screens.

• When multiple FABs existed on the same screen, they sometimes overlapped.
Initially, I assumed this was a minor styling issue related to position: 'absolute'.
However, the root cause was something else entirely.
The Root Cause: absolute Positioning Is Relative to the Parent, Not the Screen
My first implementation followed a common approach:
<View style={styles.container}>
{/* Screen content */}
<TouchableOpacity style={styles.fab}>
<Icon name="add" />
</TouchableOpacity>
</View>fab: {
position: 'absolute',
bottom: 24,
right: 16,
}Here’s the key detail:
In React Native, position: 'absolute' is relative to the parent component, not the screen itself.
As the height of the parent changes, the FAB’s position changes as well.
For example:

• The button may shift when the content inside a ScrollView grows.

• It may appear higher when the content is short.

• Safe area values differ between devices.

• Bottom tab bar heights vary depending on the platform and device.
As a result, the same FAB can behave inconsistently across devices.
The Solution: FAB + Portal
Eventually, I decided to use two components from React Native Paper together:

• FAB

• Portal
import { FAB, Portal } from 'react-native-paper';<Portal>
<FAB
icon="add"
onPress={handlePress}
style={styles.fab}
/>
</Portal>
The key piece here is Portal.
Portal removes the component from its parent hierarchy and renders it at the top layer of the application.
This means the FAB:

• Is no longer affected by ScrollView

• Does not depend on parent height

• Works independently of content length

• Uses the same reference point across all screens
In short, the FAB is no longer rendered inside the screen itself but at the application’s top render layer.
Safe Area Support
Using Portal alone wasn't enough.
On iPhone models, the FAB still needed to account for the home indicator area.
That’s why I used react-native-safe-area-context:
const insets = useSafeAreaInsets();This ensured that:

• Android devices didn’t have unnecessary spacing

• The FAB no longer disappeared behind the home indicator on iPhones
One More Thing to Watch Out For
In bottom tab navigators, screens are often kept mounted instead of being unmounted.
As a result, FABs from inactive tabs can remain visible.
This happens because Portal works at the application level rather than the screen level.
To solve this, I used useIsFocused():
const isFocused = useIsFocused();
if (!isFocused) {
return null;
}This ensures that the FAB is rendered only when its screen is active.
Final Architecture
The final solution looks like this:

• All FAB logic is centralized in a single component

• Position calculations are managed in one place

• Safe area support is built in

• The render layer is separated using Portal

• Active screen visibility is controlled with useIsFocused()
Now, adding a FAB to a new screen is as simple as this:
<FABButton
type="text"
onPress={handlePress}
/>Everything else is handled internally.
This experience reminded me of an important lesson:
In mobile development, some problems initially look like simple styling issues.
But often, the real issue is that the component is being rendered in the wrong layer.
